source: The 2026 Core Content of Emergency Medical Services Medicine

rows.title: Physician directed care by radio or phone
Determination of medical necessity in the field
Assessment of personnel competence and fitness for duty (initial and continued)
Prioritization of response (e.g., determining local needs based on local resources)
EMS clinician levels (EMR, EMT, AEMT, Paramedic)
Critical care and specialty care transport
Airline, cruise ship, and resort medical support
EMS Personnel Health, Safety, and Wellness

Regulations and standards (e.g., National Fire Protection Association [NFPA] 1582, Ryan White Act, Occupational Safety and Health Administration [OSHA] requirements, National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health [NIOSH])
Prevention and intervention for psychologically stressful events
Working with government and public health agencies
Local laws and ordinances impacting EMS
Federal laws and regulations impacting EMS
Controlled Substance Act (and Protecting Patient Access to Emergency Medications Act)
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
Specialty hospital designations and patient transport
Integration of EMS, community public resources, mental health resources, and social services
Public information and interacting with the media
Termination of resuscitation in the field
Mechanical CPR and real-time feedback devices
Withholding resuscitation (including determination of death and applying advanced directives)
Airway management in challenging conditions (e.g., low light, atypical position)
Access or placement of central venous lines
Placement of intraosseous lines (adult and pediatric)
Goal of minimizing scene time for severe trauma
Management and resuscitation of multi-trauma patient
Prehospital administration of thrombolytics for STEMI
Application of backboard as extrication device
Splinting, including use of nontraditional materials
Use of capnometry and capnography waveforms in diagnosis and management
Management of respiratory distress or shortness of breath
Acute Coronary Syndrome, including ST-elevation myocardial infarct (STEMI)
Utilization of electrocardiogram (ECG) in the field
Prehospital administration of thrombolytics for STEMI
Field identification of heart failure (acute or chronic)
Pericardiocentesis with or without ultrasound or other guidance device
Prehospital stroke identification and severity scales
Systems of care (e.g., levels of stroke centers)
Use of dialysis access for resuscitation
High-risk delivery of a fetus (e.g., preterm delivery)
Management of abnormal presentations of fetus (e.g., breech)
Effect of clinical diagnosis on transport decisions
Clinical Management in Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear, Explosive (CBRNE) Incidents
Chemical (including nerve agents, blister agents/vesicants, asphyxiants, irritants/corrosives, riot control agents)
Contact (e.g., Norovirus, MRSA, Hep B, C)
Novel respiratory viruses (e.g., H5N1, MERS, COVID-19)
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) guidelines for blood-borne pathogens
"Identify, Isolate, and Inform" for high consequence infectious diseases (e.g. Ebola Virus Disease)
Recognition of a communicable disease or biothreat (signs, symptoms, travel or exposure history)
Isolation of persons who pose a communicable disease risk
Health monitoring, post-exposure prophylaxis, and testing
Infection control liaison between hospital and EMS agency
Cleaning and disinfection of the ambulance
Methods to cool a patient in the field
High altitude injury (e.g., high altitude pulmonary edema, high altitude cerebral edema)
Effect of altitude on patient management
Effect of altitude on EMS personnel
Children with special healthcare needs (e.g., technology dependent)
Advanced directives (e.g., do not resuscitate [DNR], do not intubate [DNI], physician orders for life sustaining treatment [POLST], living wills)
Management of bystanders while caring for patient
Types (e.g., intimate partner, sexual, elder, child)
Patients with Limited Language Proficiency/Cultural Humility
Graphic display tools (run chart and control chart)
Local, state, and national EMS data sources (including registries and databases)
Systematic literature reviews with or without meta-analyses
Ethical frameworks (e.g., Belmont Report, Common Rule, Final Rule)
Role of the Institutional Review Board
Human subjects research and vulnerable populations
Observational studies (e.g., cohort, case control, case series, case report)
Respective roles of law enforcement, EMS, hospitals, emergency management, and public health
State and federal criteria for disaster declaration
Contingency and crisis standards of care
Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear, Explosive (CBRNE) Incident Management
Care of the contaminated patient while wearing PPE
Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs) and terrorist activity
Ingress/egress of personnel, patients, and equipment
National Special Security Events (NSSE) and Special Event Assessment Rating (SEAR) Events
Onsite public safety answering point and dispatch
Role of tactical teams in high threat environment
Military vs. civilian tactical medical approaches
Medical care in a high threat environment
Medical support of tactical team members
TECHNICAL RESCUE AND URBAN SEARCH AND RESCUE
Medical care considerations (e.g., limited patient assessment and prolonged field care)
Medical team integration into task force operations
Health and medical support of a task force team
Patient care in an austere setting
Care and reassessment during prolonged evacuation
Field reduction of dislocations, subluxations, or fractures
Resources (e.g., equipment, medications, or point-of-care testing)
